title:
@pauliusztin_: I spent 18 months turning my Second Brain into my living research memory

Here a...
rawContent:
Tweet by @pauliusztin_ (Paul Iusztin)
Author bio: Senior AI Engineer • Founder @ Decoding AI • Author @ LLM Engineer's Handbook ~ I ship AI products and teach you about the process.
Text: I spent 18 months turning my Second Brain into my living research memory

Here are the top 5 design choices that made this possible only via skills + files, on top of my 11,563 notes (growing with ~250 files per month):

- Combine deep research algorithms with LLM knowledge bases (wikis) that run on top of your Second Brain. Like this, you can easily retrieve the right notes + have a living research object you can query yourself or pass to your agents.

- Instead of creating a global wiki that is hard to maintain and creates scale problems, create a mini wiki for each project. Like this, you don't care much about long-term issues such as stale notes, latency, clashes between notes or scale.

- Have a data strategy on how you pipe your data into your Second Brain and how you organize it. For example, I use Tiago's Forte model based on CODE + PARA. It's simple, beautiful and flexible. 

- Resist the urge to complicate it with infrastructure such as vector/graph databases, semantic search and all those "good practices". The whole system is designed using hierarchical references, so everything runs on progressive disclosure. Minimal setup, runs out of the box without hosting. 

- Tech stack: Claude, Obsidian, Readwise (keep it simple!)

I'm working on this system every day to research AI and write code or content. Works like a charm.

I'm submitting this week, together with @Whats_AI, a 40-minute video at the @aiDotEngineer conference with all the details on how this runs in practice

Meanwhile, you can check out the repo: https://t.co/JQPXAF9tIC
